chunk_summary: This section outlines tele-nutrition strategies for adolescent anti-stigma counseling and eating disorder risk screening in India, emphasizing confidentiality, consent, and urgent referral protocols for high-risk cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of confidentiality and consent in adolescent tele-nutrition pathways.
Learn to screen for eating disorder risk using the SCOFF questionnaire and interpret results.
Identify urgent referral triggers for adolescents with severe eating disorder symptoms.
Explore the role of child data protection norms in tele-nutrition practices.
key_facts: Indian telemedicine guidelines require parental/legal guardian consent for minors (<18 years).
The SCOFF questionnaire is a validated five-item screen; a score ≥2 indicates potential eating disorder risk.
Urgent referral triggers include syncope, rapid weight loss, purging behaviors, bradycardia, severe anxiety/depression, or suicidal ideation.
Child data protection norms prohibit tracking, behavioral monitoring, and targeted advertising directed at minors.
Tele weight-management tools can inadvertently encourage restrictive behaviors in adolescents.
reference_quote: The SCOFF questionnaire is a validated five-item screen; a score ≥2 should trigger further evaluation. (BMJ)
document_key_topics: Eating disorder risk screening using SCOFF
Urgent referral protocols for high-risk cases
